1: YHWH's command to send spies: shellaḥ-lˀka ˀanashim (שְׁלַח-לְךָ אֲנָשִׁים) — 'send for yourself men.' The lˀka (לְךָ, to/for yourself) is a reflexive/middle form ('send, for your own sake/benefit'). Deuteronomy 1:22 places the initiative with the people. Both accounts are true: the people proposed; YHWH permitted. The permission accommodates human desire without guaranteeing its wisdom.
2: nˀsiˀ (נָשִׂיא, leader/prince) — one from each ancestral tribe. The root nasaˀ = to lift, bear, carry. The leader is the one who carries the people. The etymology recurs throughout Numbers: the nˀsiˀim are those who bear the weight of their tribes. The twelve spies are the tribes' representative burden-bearers — their report will determine the fate of those they carry.
3: They were sent from midbar Paran (מִדְבַּר פָּארָן, wilderness of Paran). The wilderness is not merely geography; it is the theological space between promise and possession. They are sent from the wilderness toward the land — the trajectory of Israel's entire existence is contained in this mission.
4: The twelve names are given in Numbers 13:4-15. The list represents the full tribal structure of Israel. The spy mission is a census of trust: can all twelve tribes maintain faith at the border of the promise? The answer (two faithful, ten faithless) will determine the shape of the next forty years.
5: The spies from Simeon, Judah, Issachar, Ephraim, Benjamin — each listed with father's name. The patronymics ground the mission in Israel's genealogical structure. These are not anonymous agents but men with names and families, embedded in the covenant community whose future their report will determine.
6: Calev ben Yephunneh (כָּלֵב בֶּן-יְפֻנֶּה) — the Calebite, from Judah. Caleb will emerge as the defining contrast to the faithless spies. His name may derive from kelev (dog) or from a root meaning 'heart/wholeness.' The later idiom for his faithfulness will be malaˀ ˀaḥarei YHWH (filled after/fully followed YHWH, Numbers 14:24) — his whole heart followed.
7: The spy from Issachar: Igal son of Joseph. Each tribe sends its best representative — those whom Moses knew to be leaders (11:16 used the same naˀ selection). The mission is the highest-stakes reconnaissance in Israel's history; the scouts are the top tier of tribal leadership.
8: Hoshea son of Nun from Ephraim — the man whose name will be changed in Numbers 13:16. The future great leader of Israel appears in this list unremarkably, embedded among eleven others. Greatness in Israel's narrative is regularly first seen as ordinary.
9: The spy from Benjamin: Palti son of Raphu. The Benjaminite slot recalls the tribe that will later produce Saul (Israel's first king) and Paul (apostle to the Gentiles). Here the representative carries no such significance — he is simply one of twelve bearing the common mission.
10: The spy from Zebulun: Gaddiel son of Sodi. Zebulun's territory (later the tribal homeland of Jesus' Galilean ministry, Isaiah 9:1; Matthew 4:13-16) is represented here at the moment of the great failure of nerve. The land that the Messiah will honor with his presence is scouted by those who will declare it unconquerable.
11: The spy from Manasseh (Joseph): Gaddi son of Susi. The Joseph tribes (Ephraim and Manasseh) each send a representative — they are counted as two half-tribes. The double portion of Joseph's inheritance is already structurally embedded in the spy roster.
12: The spy from Dan: Ammiel son of Gemalli. Dan will later settle in the far north (Judges 18). The scout of Dan's territory in Canaan represents a tribe whose eventual home is about as far from the Negev as possible. The whole land is being surveyed by the representative of the whole people.
13: The spy from Asher: Sethur son of Michael. Asher's tribal territory will include the coastal plain and the mountains of the north — among the most productive land in Canaan. The fruit the spies bring back (Numbers 13:23) likely comes from areas Asher will eventually inhabit.
14: The spy from Naphtali: Nahbi son of Vophsi. Naphtali's territory in the north (around the Sea of Galilee) will be Zebulun's neighbor. Both are from the region Jesus will inhabit; both are scouted by men who will come back faithless.
15: The spy from Gad: Geuel son of Machi. Gad will ultimately settle east of the Jordan (Num 32) — ironically, Gad's representative scouts a land the tribe will choose not to inhabit. The irony threads through the spy narrative: even those who see the land's goodness may not enter it.
16: wayiqraˀ Mosheh lehosheˁa bin-Nun Yehoshuˁa (וַיִּקְרָא מֹשֶׁה לְהוֹשֵׁעַ בִּן-נוּן יְהוֹשֻׁעַ) — Moses renamed Hoshea son of Nun as Joshua. Hoshea (הוֹשֵׁעַ) = 'salvation' (from yashaˁ). Yehoshuˁa (יְהוֹשֻׁעַ) = 'YHWH saves / YHWH is salvation' — the divine name added to the root. This is precisely the name of Jesus in Hebrew: Yeshua (יֵשׁוּעַ) is the contracted form of the same name. When Moses gave Joshua his name, he gave him the name of the one who would be the true leader into the true promised land.
17: Moses sends them into the Negev and then the hill country: ˁalu zeh banNegev (עֲלוּ זֶה בַנֶּגֶב). The specific directional instructions frame the scouting mission as methodical intelligence gathering — not a random wander but a systematic survey. The theological and geographical dimensions of the land are to be assessed simultaneously.
18: ureˀitem ˀet-haˀarets mah-hi (וּרְאִיתֶם אֶת-הָאָרֶץ מַה-הִוא) — 'see what the land is, whether the people are strong or weak, many or few.' The spy instructions are structured as a series of questions: what is the land, what are the people, what are the cities? The survey is comprehensive — land, people, cities, soil, vegetation. The spies' failure is not that they answered incorrectly but that they drew the wrong conclusion from correct data.
19: The land is to be assessed: is it tobah hˀ ˀim raˁah (טוֹבָה הִוא אִם-רָעָה, good or bad)? shamenim ˀim-razim (שְׁמֵנִים אִם-רָזִים, fat/fertile or lean)? The categories are agricultural and military. Moses wants the full picture — the positive and the negative. The spies will provide exactly this; their failure is in the weight they assign to each category.
20: hˀamets ˀet-ˀanashim (וְהִתְחַזַּקְתֶּם) — 'be bold' (from ḥazaq, to be strong). The same root as the repeated commissions to Joshua: ḥazaq weˀemats (be strong and courageous, Joshua 1:6,7,9,18). Moses already calls them to the courage that Joshua will need to embody permanently. bikkurei ˁanabim (בִּכּוּרֵי עֲנָבִים, first fruits of grapes) — the timing of the mission: the season of first-ripe grapes means maximum display of the land's fruitfulness.
21: They surveyed from the wilderness of Zin to Rehob (in the far north): meˀarets Zin ˁad-Rehov. The full north-south extent of the land — from the Negev to the Syrian border — is covered. The survey is exhaustive; no part of the promised territory is unexamined. The faithless report is not a product of incomplete information.
22: weˀaḥiman Sheshai weTalmay yaldey haˀanaq — they saw the Anakim (descendants of Anak) in Hebron. The Anakim are the first appearance of the giant-warrior threat that will recur in the spy report. Hebron is specifically mentioned — the very city associated with Abraham's burial place (Gen 23) and David's early capital. The land of the covenant is inhabited by giants.
23: eshkol ˁanabim ˀeḥad (אֶשְׁכּוֹל עֲנָבִים אֶחָד, a single cluster of grapes) carried on a pole by two men. Eshkol (cluster) names the valley (Nahal Eshkol, Valley of the Cluster). The image of two men carrying a single grape cluster on a pole between them has often been read typologically: the cluster as a type of Christ's fruitfulness (John 15:5), and the carrying between two men as a figure of the cross (the pole between the two bearers). The pomegranates and figs complete the three-fruit witness to the land's abundance.
24: The valley is named Eshkol from ˀeshkol (cluster). Place-naming from events is the standard Israelite aetiological pattern (as at Taberah, Kibroth-hattaavah). The land names its own fruitfulness; the geography testifies to the promise's truth even when the people deny it.
25: miqets arbaˁim yom (מִקֵּץ אַרְבָּעִים יוֹם) — after forty days. The forty-day period will become forty years of wandering (Numbers 14:34 — a year for each day). The proportion between the scouting period and the punishment period makes the wandering a measured divine response: each day of faithless observation becomes a year of wilderness existence.
26: The spies return to Moses, Aaron, and the whole congregation at Kadesh in the wilderness of Paran. wayashivu ˀotam davarin (וַיָּשִׁיבוּ אוֹתָם דָּבָר) — 'they brought back a word/report to them.' The davar (word, report) is the pivot of the narrative. The same word: the land will be characterized by the word spoken about it, not only by its objective reality.
27: zavet ḥalav udˀvash (זָבַת חָלָב וּדְבַשׁ, flowing with milk and honey) — the land is exactly what YHWH promised. The spies confirm the divine description (Ex Numbers 3:8). we-ˀefes (וְאֶפֶס — but, nevertheless, however) introduces the reversal: everything good that was promised is true AND (the adversative conjunction of fear) the inhabitants are powerful. The truth of the promise and the reality of the obstacle are both confirmed; the spies fail in the third step — drawing the conclusion of faith.
28: ˁarim betsurot gedolot meˀod (עָרִים בְּצֻרוֹת גְּדֹלוֹת מְאֹד, large, fortified cities, very great) — the report of military strength. The fortifications are real. The grasshopper report is not based on false intelligence; it is a theological failure of inference. Correct data + wrong theology = faithless conclusion.
29: Geographic distribution of the enemies: Amalek in the Negev, Hittites/Jebusites/Amorites in the hill country, Canaanites by the sea and the Jordan. The enemy list is comprehensive — every direction, every terrain type is occupied by someone powerful. The land is surrounded; there is no unguarded approach. From a purely human military assessment, the situation is hopeless.
30: wayyas Kalev ˀet-haˁam (וַיַּהַס כָּלֵב אֶת-הָעָם) — 'Caleb silenced the people.' Hush (הוּשׁ) = to silence, hush. Caleb's intervention is a single act of faith against a rising tide of despair. ˁaloh naˁaleh (עָלֹה נַעֲלֶה, we shall certainly go up) — the infinitive absolute + imperfect = emphatic certainty. 'We can certainly take it' is the only grammatically emphatically positive statement in the entire spy report.
31: loˀ nukhal laˁalot (לֹא נוּכַל לַעֲלוֹת, we are not able to go up) — the direct counter to Caleb's emphatic ˁaloh naˁaleh. The same verb (ˁalah, to go up) but with loˀ nukhal (we cannot) rather than the emphatic infinitive absolute. Caleb's 'we certainly can' meets the majority's 'we certainly cannot.' The theological question is not military but about the nature of YHWH's power and promise.
32: dibbat haˀarets (דִּבַּת הָאָרֶץ, evil report of the land) — literally 'the whispering/slander of the land.' Dibbah is the word for slander, false report, defamation. The ten spies' report is characterized as dibbah — not merely mistaken assessment but a false accusation against the land (and ultimately against YHWH who promised it). ˀerets ˀokhelet yoshˁeha (land that devours its inhabitants) — a land characterized as consuming those who live in it; the exact opposite of the milk-and-honey description.
33: Nephilim (נְפִילִים) — from naphal (to fall). The same word as Genesis 6:4. The connection to the pre-flood giants frames the Anakim as a pre-diluvian category of threat — something that survived the flood. ḥagavim (חֲגָבִים, grasshoppers/locusts) — the self-description of the spies. The word is used for locusts (Leviticus 11:22; Qoh Numbers 12:5; Isaiah 40:22). To describe themselves as grasshoppers before the Anakim is to describe themselves as prey. weˀen ˀanaḥnu bˀeyneyhem (וְכֵן הָיִינוּ בְּעֵינֵיהֶם, we seemed so in their eyes) — the psychological self-diminishment becomes the assumed perception of the enemy. Fear projects its smallness onto the adversary.
